summary: Hotz argues that Ethereum's original vision of being 'the world's computer' was brilliant but has been abandoned with the shift to Proof of Stake. He identifies databases as the key infrastructure that gives big tech its power, noting that standing one up remains frustratingly difficult in 2024. He calculates that a true world computer would need to handle roughly 10 million transactions per second—a million times more than Ethereum's current 13 TPS. He suggests that once a single machine can handle all global transactions, big tech's dominance will collapse.
key_points: Ethereum lost its way by moving to Proof of Stake instead of focusing on building a practical world computer
Database infrastructure is the real moat protecting big tech companies
The world fundamentally only needs one database—multiple instances exist for throughput and sandboxing, not necessity
Global transaction volume is approximately 10 million TPS when aggregating credit cards, social media, email, and other services
Ethereum's 13 TPS is off by a factor of one million from what's actually needed
money_quotes: In 2024, it's still painfully annoying to stand up a database.
This is the only thing big tech really has, to the point that I often use Google Sheets or a GitHub repo effectively as a database.
Another fact about databases is that the world only needs one of them.
One properly built database can kill big tech.
Ethereum is off by a factor of a million.
Once a single machine can handle the worlds transactions, big tech falls.
key_insight: Big tech's real competitive advantage is simply making databases easy to use, and a sufficiently capable decentralized world computer handling 10 million TPS would eliminate that moat entirely.
